blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلْمُرْسَلَـٰتِ عُرْفًا (١)
By the [angelic] emissaries sent successively, (1)
فَٱلْعَـٰصِفَـٰتِ عَصْفًا (٢)
by those who sweep along like gale, (2)
وَٱلنَّـٰشِرَٰتِ نَشْرًا (٣)
by those who publish [the Divine messages] far and wide, (3)
فَٱلْفَـٰرِقَـٰتِ فَرْقًا (٤)
by those who separate [the truth from falsehood] distinctly, (4)
فَٱلْمُلْقِيَـٰتِ ذِكْرًا (٥)
by those who inspire [God’s] remembrance, (5)
عُذْرًا أَوْ نُذْرًا (٦)
as exemption or warning: (6)
إِنَّمَا تُوعَدُونَ لَوَٰقِعٌ (٧)
what you are promised will surely befall. (7)
فَإِذَا ٱلنُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ (٨)
When the stars are blotted out (8)
وَإِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ فُرِجَتْ (٩)
and the sky is cleft, (9)
وَإِذَا ٱلْجِبَالُ نُسِفَتْ (١٠)
when the mountains are scattered [like dust] (10)
وَإِذَا ٱلرُّسُلُ أُقِّتَتْ (١١)
and time is set for the apostles [to bear witness] (11)
لِأَىِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ (١٢)
—for what day has [all] that been set [to occur]? (12)
لِيَوْمِ ٱلْفَصْلِ (١٣)
For the Day of Judgement! (13)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ (١٤)
And what will show you what is the Day of Judgement!? (14)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١٥)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (15)

Content of Surah al-Mursalat

It is well-known that this Surah was revealed in "Makkah," although some have explicitly stated that verse 48, "And when it is said to them, 'Bow [in prayer],' they do not bow," is "Madani," even though they have mentioned no clear evidence for this. If the issue of "Ruku" (bowing) and "Salah" (prayer) is the basis for this inference, it does not seem correct, as Muslims performed prayer and Ruku in Makkah for a long time.

In any case, the primary subject matter discussed in this Surah concerns issues related to the Resurrection and the threats and warnings directed at the deniers and rejecters.

One of the distinctive features of this Surah is that the verse:

"Woe, that Day, to the deniers" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ)

is repeated ten times, each time following a new topic.

After mentioning several oaths, it informs of the Resurrection and the heavy and difficult events of the Rising, followed by this very verse, stating: "Woe to the deniers on that Day!" In the next stage, it recounts the sorrowful fate of past sinful nations.

In the third stage, it touches upon aspects of the characteristics of human creation.

In the fourth stage, it describes some of the divine blessings on Earth.

In the fifth stage, it details parts of the punishment of the deniers.

Furthermore, at each stage, it makes a reference to an awakening and soul-stirring matter, followed by the repetition of this verse. It even mentions the heavenly blessings granted to the pious in one section, so as to blend warning with glad tidings and threat with encouragement.

In any case, this repetition brings to mind the repetition of certain verses in Surah al-Rahman, with the difference being that there the discourse was about blessings, while here it is mostly about the punishments of the deniers.

The name "Al-Mursalat" was chosen for this Surah in accordance with its first verse.

The Virtue of Reciting This Surah

In a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Mursalat, it will be recorded that he is not among the polytheists." 1 And in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated:

"Whoever recites it, Allah will introduce him to Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) [and place them in proximity]." [2] Undoubtedly, this reward and virtue belong to those who recite, reflect, and act upon it. Hence, it is mentioned in a Hadith that some companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to him: "How quickly the signs of old age have appeared in you, O Messenger of Allah!"

1 and [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 414.

He replied:

"Surahs Hud, al-Waqi'ah, al-Mursalat, and 'Amma Yatasa'alun have made me old!" 1 It is noteworthy that in all these Surahs, the states of the Resurrection and the terrifying matters of that great tribunal are reflected, and it was these that affected the holy soul of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Naturally, recitation without reflection and a decision to act cannot produce such an effect.

1: Al-Khisal by Saduq, "Chapter of the Four," Hadith 10.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ

By those [winds] sent forth in succession (1) And those that move as a violent tempest (2) And by those that spread and scatter (3) And those that separate [the right from the wrong] (4)

And those that deliver a reminder (5) As justification or warning (6) Indeed, what you are promised is certain to happen (7) So when the stars are obliterated (8) And when the heaven is split open (9)

And when the mountains are blown away (10) And when the messengers are brought to their appointed time (11) For what Day was this deferred? (12) For the Day of Judgement (13) And what can make you know what is the Day of Judgement? (14)

Woe, that Day, to the deniers (15)

Commentary

Divine Promises are True; Woe to the Deniers!

At the beginning of this Surah, as an introduction in the first five verses, five oaths are mentioned, regarding the interpretation of which much has been said:

He says: "By those [sent ones] who are sent forth in succession" (وَ الْمُرْسَلاتِ عُرْفاً) 1

"And those that move like a storm and a violent wind" (فَالْعاصِفاتِ عَصْفاً).

"And by those that spread and scatter" (وَ النَّاشِراتِ نَشْراً).

"And those that separate" (فَالْفارِقاتِ فَرْقاً).

"And those that deliver awakening verses" (فَالْمُلْقِياتِ ذِكْراً).

"As justification or warning" (عُذْراً أَوْ نُذْراً) 1. Now let us see what these cryptic oaths, which inform of important matters, signify.

There are three well-known interpretations here:

1- All these five parts refer to "winds and storms," which play an effective role in many matters of the natural world. Thus, the meaning of the verses would be: By the winds that blow one after another.

And by the storms that move violently.

And by those that spread the clouds and send forth life-giving drops of rain from within them toward dry lands.

And by those that scatter the clouds after the rain has fallen.

And by these winds that, through this program, remind humans of God.

(Some have also considered "فَالْعاصِفاتِ عَصْفاً" to refer to storms of punishment, which are the opposite of life-giving winds, and which in turn serve as a source of reminder and awakening).

2- All these oaths refer to the "angels of heaven," meaning: By the angels who are sent successively to the Prophets (or the angels sent with recognized and known programs).

And by those who, like a storm, proceed with full speed toward their mission.

1: The grammatical position of "عُذْراً أَوْ نُذْراً" is accusative (Mansub) as an object of purpose (Maf'ul lahu), and some have considered it a circumstantial adverb (Hal).

And by those who spread and disseminate the verses of the Heavenly Books before the Prophets.

And those who, by this act, separate truth from falsehood.

And those who deliver the remembrance of the Truth and His commands to the Prophets.

3- The first and second oaths refer to the matter of "winds and storms," while the third, fourth, and fifth oaths refer to the dissemination of the verses of Truth by "angels," then the separation of truth from falsehood, and subsequently the delivery of the remembrance and divine commands to the Prophets for the purpose of justification and warning.

What can serve as evidence for the third interpretation is, firstly, the separation of these two sets of oaths in the verses by the conjunction "Waw" (and), while the rest are joined by "Fa," which indicates their connection and sequence.

Secondly, as we shall see, all these oaths are for the matter mentioned in the seventh verse—namely, the truth and reality of the Resurrection and the Hereafter. We know that on the threshold of the Resurrection, a great transformation occurs in the world: violent storms, earthquakes, and soul-stirring events on one hand, followed by the establishment of the Divine Court of Justice on the other, where angels spread the records of deeds, separate the ranks of believers and disbelievers, and deliver the Divine Decree in this regard.

If the five parts above are explained according to this interpretation, they will have a harmony with the Muqsam bihi (that which is sworn by), and the latter interpretation is superior in this regard.

The "Remembrance" (Dhikr) in the phrase "فَالْمُلْقِياتِ ذِكْراً" refers either to the sciences and knowledge delivered to the Prophets or the verses revealed to them. We know that in the verses of the Quran, the Quran itself is referred to as "Dhikr," as in verse 6 of Surah al-Hijr:

"And they said, 'O you upon whom the message (Dhikr) has been sent down, indeed you are mad!'" The use of "Al-Mulqiyat" in the plural form, even though the Angel of Revelation, Gabriel, was a single individual, is because some narrations suggest that sometimes great groups of angels accompanied Gabriel for the revelation of Quranic verses. In verse 15 of Surah 'Abasa, it is also stated: "By the hands of messengers (angels)," meaning the Quranic verses were revealed to the Prophet of Islam by the hands of divine messengers (angels).

Now, for what purpose are these oaths? The next verse unveils this meaning, saying: "Indeed, what you are promised is certain to happen" (إِنَّما تُوعَدُونَ لَواقِعٌ). The Resurrection and the Rising, reward and punishment, reckoning and recompense—all are true, and there is no doubt in them.

Some have also taken it to refer to all divine promises, whether those given to the good or the wicked in this world or the hereafter, but the subsequent verses show that the intended meaning is specifically the promise of the Resurrection 1. Although no argument for the issue of the Hereafter is presented in this verse and it settles for the claim alone, the beauty of the matter lies in the fact that the preceding oaths contain matters that are themselves proofs of the Hereafter. Among these is the reviving of dead lands through the falling of rain, which is itself an example of the scene of the Resurrection. Furthermore, the revelation of divine obligations upon the Prophets and the sending of messengers, which would be meaningless without the existence of the Hereafter, shows that the promise of the Resurrection is certain.

1: The conjunction with "Fa" also strengthens this point.

A similar point is mentioned in verse 23 of Surah al-Dhariyat, where Allah says:

"So by the Lord of the heaven and earth, indeed, it is truth." The oath by the "Lord" (Rab), which refers to the Lordship of the Almighty and His management of the world of creation, necessitates that He does not leave His servants without sustenance.

Then, He proceeds to describe the signs of this promised Day, saying: "This promised Day will arrive when the stars are obliterated and darkened from the face of the sky" (فَإِذَا النُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ).

"And the heaven (the celestial bodies) is split open" (وَ إِذَا السَّماءُ فُرِجَتْ).

"And when the mountains are uprooted" (وَ إِذَا الْجِبالُ نُسِفَتْ).

"Tumisat" is from the root "Tams," meaning to erase and eliminate the traces of something. Here, it may refer to the obliteration of the light of the stars or their disintegration, but the first interpretation is more appropriate, as stated in verse 2 of Surah al-Takwir: "And when the stars fall, losing their light." "Nusifat" is from the root "Nasf," which originally means pouring food grains into a sieve and shaking it to separate the husk from the grain. Here, it means the crushing and then the blowing away of the mountains by the wind.

Fundamentally, it is understood from numerous verses in the Holy Quran that the end of this world is accompanied by a series of terrifying and crushing events, such that its system is completely disintegrated, and the world of the Hereafter is replaced by a new system.

These events are so strange and horrific that they cannot be described by any words. Are events that uproot mountains and smash them together until they turn into dust and become like carded wool describable?

As some commentators have expressed, the magnitude of these events is such that the greatest earthquakes witnessed by man are like small firecrackers exploded by children for play compared to the largest atomic bomb.

In any case, these Quranic expressions are evidence of the vast difference between the systems of the Hereafter and the systems of this world.

Following that, He refers to the scene of the Resurrection, adding: "And this is when the time is appointed for the messengers to come in turn and give their testimony regarding the nations" (وَ إِذَا الرُّسُلُ أُقِّتَتْ) 1. As stated in verse 6 of Surah al-A'raf: "Then We will surely question those to whom [a message] was sent, and We will surely question the messengers."

1: "Uqqitat" was originally "Wuqqitat" from the root "Waqt" (time), where the initial "Waw" was changed to a "Hamza." It means "appointing a time" for the messengers of the Lord. It is also clear that the time is not appointed for them personally, but for their action—namely, their testimony; hence, it is said that the verse has an implied elision.

Then He adds: "For what Day has the testimony of these messengers and their witnessing over the nations been deferred?" (لِأَيِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ) 1

"For the Day of Sorting" (لِيَوْمِ الْفَصْلِ). The day of separating truth from falsehood, separating the ranks of believers from disbelievers, and the righteous from the wicked, and the day of absolute judgment of Truth regarding everyone.

This question and answer are to express the magnitude of that great Day, and what a meaningful and eloquent expression has been used for that day: "The Day of Sortings!"

Then, to express the magnitude of that Day, He says: "And what can make you know what the Day of Sorting is?" (وَ ما أَدْراكَ ما يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ). When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, with that vast and extensive knowledge and that sharp vision that observed the secrets of the unseen, does not fully know the dimensions of the magnitude of that Day, the situation of the rest of the people is clear. As we have said many times, understanding all the magnificent secrets of the Resurrection is not possible for us, the prisoners in the cage of this world; we only see a shadow of it and believe in it.

1: According to this interpretation, the pronoun in "Ujjilat" refers back to the testimony of the Prophets and messengers, which is understood from the previous verse. However, some have said it refers to all matters related to the Prophets and the news they gave regarding reward, punishment, the events of the Resurrection, and the like. Some also consider it a reference to all the matters mentioned in the previous verses, such as the darkening of the stars, etc. But it is clear that the first interpretation is the most appropriate, as the referent of the pronoun is connected to it.

In the final verse under discussion, He severely threatens the deniers of that Day, saying: "Woe, that Day, to the deniers" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ).

Some have interpreted "Wayl" as "destruction," some as "various types of punishment," and some as "a valley full of punishment in Hell." This word is usually used for tragic events, and here it recounts the painful fate of the deniers on that Day 1. The "deniers" here refers to those who deny the Resurrection. We know that one who does not believe in the Resurrection, the Court of Justice of Allah, and the reckoning and recompense, will easily commit any kind of sin, oppression, and corruption. However, firm faith in that Day grants a person commitment, a sense of responsibility, piety, and God-wariness.

Key Point: 1- The Content of These Oaths!

In the above verses, oaths are first taken by the winds and storms, and this is because of the important role they play in the world of creation: they move the clouds, then join them over dry and dead lands, and after the rain falls, they disperse them.

They move the seeds of plants and create forests and pastures.

They fertilize many flowers and fruits, and they carry heat and cold from

1: A more detailed explanation regarding the meaning of "Wayl" and its difference from "Ways" and "Wayh" is provided in Vol. 22, p. 402, under verse 60 of Surah al-Dhariyat.

different regions of the earth to other points, moderating the air.

They carry fresh, oxygen-rich air from fields and deserts to cities, and send polluted air to the deserts for purification.

They make the sea waters turbulent and wavy, making them oxygen-rich for aquatic living beings. Yes, winds and breezes perform great and vital services in the world.

The other part of these oaths, which speaks of the program of the revelation of inspiration through angels, also bears a resemblance in the spiritual world to the blowing of the breeze in the material world. The angels bring down the words of revelation—which are like life-giving drops of rain—upon the hearts of the Prophets of God, and they cause the flowers and fruits of divine knowledge and piety to grow in the hearts. In this way, Allah has sworn by both the educators of the material world and the educators of the spiritual world.

Interestingly, all these oaths are to express the reality of the Day when all these efforts and endeavors bear fruit: the Day of Resurrection and the Day of Sorting.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ نُهْلِكِ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٦)
Did We not destroy the former generations, (16)
ثُمَّ نُتْبِعُهُمُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١٧)
[and] then made the latter ones follow them? (17)
كَذَٰلِكَ نَفْعَلُ بِٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (١٨)
That is how We deal with the guilty. (18)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١٩)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (19)
أَلَمْ نَخْلُقكُّم مِّن مَّآءٍ مَّهِينٍ (٢٠)
Have We not created you from a base fluid, (20)
فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ فِى قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ (٢١)
[and] then lodged it in a secure abode (21)
إِلَىٰ قَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (٢٢)
until a known span [of time]? (22)
فَقَدَرْنَا فَنِعْمَ ٱلْقَـٰدِرُونَ (٢٣)
Then We determined; and how excellent determiners We are! (23)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٢٤)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (24)
أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ ٱلْأَرْضَ كِفَاتًا (٢٥)
Have We not made the earth a receptacle (25)
أَحْيَآءً وَأَمْوَٰتًا (٢٦)
for the living and the dead, (26)
وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ شَـٰمِخَـٰتٍ وَأَسْقَيْنَـٰكُم مَّآءً فُرَاتًا (٢٧)
and set in it lofty [and] firm mountains, and given you agreeable water to drink? (27)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٢٨)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (28)

Commentary

With All These Manifestations of Power, Do You Still Doubt the Resurrection?!

In these verses, He warns the deniers of the Resurrection through various means and awakens them from the heavy sleep of negligence with diverse explanations.

First, He takes them by the hand and leads them through the history of the past, showing them the disaster-stricken lands of the former disbelieving nations, saying: "Did We not destroy the former peoples (who took the path of disbelief and denial)?" (أَ لَمْ نُهْلِكِ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

Their traces are visible not only on the pages of history but also upon the face of the earth.

Nations such as the people of 'Ad, Thamud, the people of Nuh, the people of Lut, and the people of Pharaoh—some were punished and destroyed for their deeds by a flood, some by a thunderbolt, some by a fierce wind, and some by earthquakes and stones from the sky.

"Then We cause the later ones (who perform similar deeds) to follow them" (ثُمَّ نُتْبِعُهُمُ الْآخِرِينَ). For this is a continuous Sunnah (divine law) that admits no discrimination or exception. Is it possible for Him to punish one group for a crime and approve of the same crime for others?!

Therefore, in the next verse, He adds: "Thus do We deal with the criminals" (كَذلِكَ نَفْعَلُ بِالْمُجْرِمِينَ). This verse, in fact, serves as an explanation of the reason for the destruction of the "former peoples" and the subsequent destruction of the "later peoples," because divine punishments are neither for the sake of seeking revenge nor for settling personal accounts; rather, they follow the principle of desert and the requirements of wisdom.

Some have said: "The former" refers to nations of the distant past, like the people of Nuh, 'Ad, and Thamud, and "the later" refers to nations that came after them, like the people of Lut and the people of Pharaoh. However, considering that "نتبعهم" (We cause to follow) is in the "present/future tense" while "أَ لَمْ نُهْلِكِ" (Did We not destroy) carries a past meaning, it becomes clear that "the former" encompasses all previous nations destroyed by divine punishment, and "the later" includes the disbelievers who were present during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, or those who come into existence after him and become tainted with crime, sin, oppression, and corruption.

Finally, drawing a conclusion, He adds: "Woe, that Day, to the deniers" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ). "That Day" (يومئذ) here refers to the Day of Resurrection, as their primary and significant punishment pertains to that day. This repetition is for emphasis. The suggestion by some that this verse refers to worldly punishment, while the similar preceding verse refers to the punishment of the Hereafter, seems very unlikely.

Then He takes them by the hand and leads them into the world of the fetus, showing them the greatness and power of Allah, and the abundance of His favors in this mysterious world, so that on one hand, they may realize Allah's power over the issue of the Resurrection and the Return, and on the other hand, consider themselves indebted to His countless blessings and bow their heads in submission at His threshold. He says: "Did We not create you from a base and insignificant fluid?!" (أَ لَمْ نَخْلُقْكُمْ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ).

"Then We placed it in a safe and prepared lodging" (فَجَعَلْناهُ فِي قَرارٍ مَكِينٍ) [1]. A lodging where all conditions for life, nourishment, growth, and protection of the human embryo have been provided from every aspect, and it is so wondrous, fascinating, and balanced that it leaves every human...

[1]: "قرار" (Qarar) means "place of settlement" and "مكين" (Makin) means "protected/secure"; it originates from "مكانت" (Makanat) which means "empowerment" (though "Makanat" sometimes also means status).

...in amazement.

Then He adds: "The placement of the embryo in that protected place continues for a known term" (إِلى‌ قَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ). A duration known to none but Allah, a period filled with many changes, transformations, and developments, where every day the embryo is clothed in a new garment of life and is advanced toward perfection in that hidden place.

Then He concludes: "We had the power to do this—to create such a noble and complete human from an insignificant, humble, and base fluid—so how excellent a Possessor of Power are We!" (فَقَدَرْنا فَنِعْمَ الْقادِرُونَ) [1] [2]. This is the same argument that the Quran repeatedly relies upon to prove the issue of the Resurrection. For instance, in the opening verses of Surah Al-Hajj, it says: How can you doubt the return of the dead to a new life when you witness His power in creating this human from an insignificant fluid, where every day of it is a resurrection and a return?! What is the difference between dust and that insignificant fluid?

[1]: The verse has an omitted part, implied as "فنعم القادرون نحن" (So how excellent are We as the Possessors of Power).

[2]: Some commentators have interpreted this verse as: "We proportioned the embryo with necessary measurements, various destinies, and characteristics in body and soul, and We are the best of proportioners." However, this interpretation seems unlikely given that the text of the Quran and the well-known recitation are without the shaddah (on the 'dal'), although some have said the triliteral root also means measuring, but in common usage, "Qadir" is not used with this meaning. (Reflect on this).

At the end, He repeats the same phrase again, saying: "Woe, that Day, to the deniers" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ). Woe to those who see all these manifestations of His power and yet deny Him.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in this regard:

"O created being who is well-proportioned, and nurtured in the darkness of wombs and multiple veils! You were started from an extract of clay and placed in a safe lodging for a known term and a destined period. You moved in your mother's womb as a fetus, unable to answer a call or hear a cry. Then you were brought out from your lodging to a home you had never witnessed, and whose paths of benefit you did not know. Who then guided you to draw nourishment from your mother's breast? And who taught you, in times of need, the places of your seeking and desire?" [1]

[1]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Sermon 163.

In another part of these verses, He proceeds to explain some of the external signs (afaqi) of Allah and His blessings and favors in the macrocosm, which serve both as proof of His power and vast mercy, and as evidence for the possibility of the Resurrection—whereas the previous verses spoke of the internal signs (anfusi) and Allah's favors in the creation of man himself.

He says: "Did We not make the earth a place of gathering for humans?!" (أَ لَمْ نَجْعَلِ الْأَرْضَ كِفاتاً) [1]

"Both for their living and their dead" (أَحْياءً وَ أَمْواتاً) [2]. "Kifat" (rhyming with Kitab) and "Kaft" (rhyming with Kashf) mean to gather and join things together. The swift flight of birds is also called "Kifat" because during fast flight, they tuck in their wings to cut through the air more quickly and move forward. The meaning is that the earth is a dwelling for all humans; it gathers the living upon itself and provides all their needs and requirements, and it also accommodates their dead. If the earth were not prepared for burying the dead, the stench and resulting diseases would create a catastrophe for all the living.

Indeed, the earth is like a mother who gathers her children around her and takes them...

[1]: "Kifatan" is the second object for "Ja'alna" and is a verbal noun used in the sense of an active participle.

[2]: "Ahya'an wa Amwatan" is a circumstantial clause (hal) for an omitted object pronoun, implied as "kifatan lakum ahya'an wa amwatan" (a gathering place for you, living and dead).

...under her wing; it accommodates humans upon itself, nurtures them, feeds them, clothes them, provides shelter, and fulfills all their needs, and it also keeps their dead in its bosom, absorbs them, and eliminates their harmful effects.

Some have interpreted "Kifat" here to mean "swift flight," considering the verse an allusion to the earth's rotation around the sun and its other movements, which were certainly not discovered at the time of the Quran's revelation.

However, considering the following verse, "living and dead," the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

Especially since it is mentioned in a narration that when the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, was returning from the battlefield of Siffin and reached the outskirts of Kufa, his blessed eyes fell upon a cemetery outside the gates of Kufa, and he said: "This is the kifat of the dead, meaning their dwellings." Then he looked at the houses of Kufa and said: "And this is the kifat of the living." Then he recited the above verses: "Did We not make the earth a place of gathering, for the living and the dead?" [1], pointing out that there is not much distance between the dwellings of the living and the dead!

Then, referring to one of the great divine blessings on the planet Earth, He adds: "And We placed therein firm, lofty mountains"

[1]: "Tafsir al-Burhan," Vol. 4, p. 417 (quoted from Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim).

(وَ جَعَلْنا فِيها رَواسِيَ شامِخاتٍ) [1]. These mountains, which reach toward the sky and whose roots are interconnected, on one hand, encompass the earth like armor, protecting it against internal pressure and pressures resulting from external tides. On the other hand, they prevent the friction of the atmosphere with the earth, gripping the air and making it rotate with the earth. Thirdly, they control great storms and winds, thus providing tranquility to the inhabitants of the earth from various aspects.

And at the end of this same verse, He points to another of the blessings of the mountains, adding:

"And We gave you sweet water to drink" (وَ أَسْقَيْناكُمْ ماءً فُراتاً). Water that is both pleasant and a source of life for you, and for your animals, crops, and gardens.

It is true that all sweet water comes from rain, but mountains play the most important role in it. Many springs and qanats gush from mountains, and the source of many great rivers and streams is the accumulated snow that sits on mountain peaks, forming the most important water reserves for humans.

Mountain peaks, due to their distance from the earth's surface, are always cold and can hold snow reserves for many years so that they gradually melt under the sun and flow as rivers.

[1]: "Rawasi" is the plural of "Rasiyah," meaning firm and stable, and "Shamikhat" is the plural of "Shamikha," meaning high/lofty. In some expressions like "Shamkha bi-anfihi," it is a metaphor for pride (Mufradat al-Raghib).

At the end of this section, He says once more: "Woe, that Day, to the deniers" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ).

The very ones who see all these signs of the Truth's power with their own eyes and witness all these divine blessings in which they are immersed, yet still deny the Resurrection and the Court of Judgment, which is the manifestation of His justice and wisdom.

Ayahs

ٱنطَلِقُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَا كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ (٢٩)
[The faithless will be told:] ‘Proceed toward what you used to deny! (29)
ٱنطَلِقُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ ظِلٍّ ذِى ثَلَـٰثِ شُعَبٍ (٣٠)
Proceed toward the triple-forked shadow, (30)
لَّا ظَلِيلٍ وَلَا يُغْنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهَبِ (٣١)
neither shady nor of any avail against the flames. (31)
إِنَّهَا تَرْمِى بِشَرَرٍ كَٱلْقَصْرِ (٣٢)
Indeed it throws up [giant] sparks like castles, (32)
كَأَنَّهُۥ جِمَـٰلَتٌ صُفْرٌ (٣٣)
[bright] like yellow camels. (33)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٣٤)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (34)
هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ لَا يَنطِقُونَ (٣٥)
This is a day wherein they will not speak, (35)
وَلَا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ (٣٦)
nor will they be permitted to offer excuses. (36)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٣٧)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (37)
هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ ۖ جَمَعْنَـٰكُمْ وَٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٣٨)
‘This is the Day of Judgement. We have brought you together with the former generations. (38)
فَإِن كَانَ لَكُمْ كَيْدٌ فَكِيدُونِ (٣٩)
If you have any stratagems [left], try them out against Me!’ (39)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٤٠)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (40)

Commentary

They Have Neither the Power to Defend Nor a Way to Escape!

In these verses, the final destiny of those who deny the Resurrection and the rejectors of that court of Divine Justice is described—a description that truly plunges a human into deep terror and clarifies the dimensions of the catastrophe.

It says: "It will be said to them: Depart immediately toward that which you used to deny!" (انْطَلِقُوا إِلى‌ ما كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تُكَذِّبُونَ‌).

Depart toward the blazing Hell that you always used to ridicule.

Move toward the various punishments that you have prepared in advance through your own deeds.

"انطلقوا" is derived from the root "inطلاق" (inطلاق), meaning to move without stopping, and it also carries a sense of being released from bonds. This is, in fact, an explanation of their situation in the arena of the Gathering (Mahshar); they are held for a long time for the accounting, then they are released and told to move toward Hell without stopping.

The speaker of these words may be Allah the Almighty addressing them directly, or the angels of punishment. In any case, the tone is laced with profound reproach, which is itself a painful and soul-crushing torment.

Then, providing more detail about this punishment, it says: "Depart toward a shadow of [the choking smoke of fire] having three columns!" (انْطَلِقُوا إِلى‌ ظِلٍّ ذِي ثَلاثِ شُعَبٍ‌). One branch from above, one from the right, and one from the left; in this manner, this thick, deadly smoke surrounds them from every side and swallows them up.

"But a shadow that is not cooling, and never protects the people of Hell from the flames of the fire" (لا ظَلِيلٍ وَ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ اللَّهَبِ‌). This is because the shadow itself arises from the fire.

The use of the word "Zill" (shadow) might create the impression that there is a shade there which slightly reduces the searing heat of the flames, but this verse strikes a line of cancellation through such a false notion, saying: This shadow is never the shadow you imagine; it is a burning and suffocating shadow, arising from the thick smoke of the fire, which can fully reflect the heat of the flames. [^1] Witness to this are the verses of Surah Al-Waqi'ah which say regarding the "Companions of the Left":

فِي سَمُومٍ وَ حَمِيمٍ وَ ظِلٍّ مِنْ يَحْمُومٍ لا بارِدٍ وَ لا كَرِيمٍ‌: "They are in the midst of scorching winds and scalding water, and in the shadow of black smoke, a shadow that is neither cool nor beneficial and comforting" (Surah Al-Waqi'ah 41-44).

Some have said these three branches are a reflection of their triple denials regarding the foundations of religion: "Monotheism" (Tawhid), "Prophethood" (Nubuwwah), and "Resurrection" (Ma'ad), for denying the Resurrection is inseparable from denying Prophethood and Monotheism.

Others have said it refers to the three sources of sin: the "power of anger" (ghadhabiyyah), "desire" (shahwiyyah), and "imagination/delusion" (wahmiyyah). Indeed, that dark smoke is an embodiment of the darkness of desires: Beware the darkness of anger and lust, For its smoke darkens the eye of the heart! When anger enters, reason departs, When passion prevails, the soul becomes bewildered!

Then, in another description of that blazing fire, it adds: "Indeed, it throws sparks like a huge palace!" (إِنَّها تَرْمِي بِشَرَرٍ كَالْقَصْرِ) [^2]

[^1]: "لا ظليل" is an adjective for "ظل" and is therefore mentioned in the genitive case (majrur).

[^2]: "شرر" (rhyming with darar) is the plural of "shararah", meaning small pieces that separate from fire and are thrown into the air, derived from the root "sharr".

Not like the sparks of the fire of this world, which are sometimes no larger than a needle's head. The term "Qasr" (palace) here is a meaningful expression. It might be thought that it would have been more appropriate to say sparks like a mountain, but it must not be forgotten that mountains—as mentioned in previous verses—are sources of various blessings and springs of sweet and fresh water. It is these palaces and castles of the oppressors that are the source of scorching flames and spark-filled fires. [^1]

In the next verse, it provides another description of the sparks and embers of this blazing fire, saying: "They are as if they were yellow camels" (كَأَنَّهُ جِمالَتٌ صُفْرٌ) [^2]. "جمالة" is the plural of "جمل" meaning "camel" (like hajar and hijarah), and "صفر" (rhyming with qufl) is the plural of "اصفر" meaning something yellow, and sometimes it is applied to dark colors inclining toward black, but here the first meaning is appropriate because the sparks of fire are yellow inclining toward red. In the previous verse, these sparks were compared to a large palace in terms of volume, and in this verse, they are compared to a group of yellow camels moving in every direction in terms of their abundance, color, speed of movement, and scattering.

[^1]: Some commentators like Fakhr al-Razi have narrated from Ibn Abbas that in the interpretation of "قصر", he said it refers to pieces of wood cut from the desert and gathered for winter, piled upon one another (it is not unlikely that this interpretation is also because the heap of piled firewood was likened to a tall palace).

[^2]: The pronoun in "كانه" may refer to "قصر" or to "شرر", but since "شرر" is plural, it is not possible without interpretation unless we consider "شرر" a collective noun.

Where the sparks are like this, it is clear what the blazing fire itself is like, and what other painful torments are placed alongside it? (May Allah protect us all from it by His mercy and grace).

Once again, at the end of this section of verses, it repeats the same warning, saying: "Woe on that Day to the deniers!" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ‌).

Then it begins another chapter of the characteristics of that terrifying Day, adding: "This is a Day they shall not speak:" (هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ‌) [^1]. Yes, on that Day, Allah places the seal of silence upon the mouths of the criminals and sinners, just as it appears in verse 65 of Surah Ya-Sin: "الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى‌ أَفْواهِهِمْ‌": "This Day We seal their mouths," and as it continues in the same verse, "their hands and feet shall speak," and even according to other verses of the Quran, their skins will find a tongue and say all that needs to be said.

Then it adds: "Nor will they be permitted to offer excuses" (وَ لا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ‌) [^2].

[^1]: Note that "يوم" here is without tanwin because it is annexed (idafah) to the concept of the sentence "لا يَنْطِقُونَ‌".

[^2]: Regarding why the phrase "فيعتذرون" appears in the nominative case (marfu') when grammatically it should be subjunctive (mansub) with the deletion of the nun, some have said it is because their failure to offer excuses is due to the fact that they have no excuse, not because of the lack of Divine permission.

They are neither permitted to speak, nor to offer excuses and defend themselves, because all realities are clear there, and they have nothing to say. Yes, this deceptive tongue that misused its freedom in the world to deny the Prophets, ridicule the saints, invalidate the truth, and make falsehood appear as truth, must be locked there as a punishment for those deeds and cease to function. This itself is a painful torment and torture—that in such a scene, a human lacks the power to defend himself or offer an excuse.

In a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "Allah is more exalted, more just, and greater than to have a servant with a valid excuse and not permit him to offer it; rather, they truly have no valid excuse to present." [^1] Of course, it is understood from some verses of the Quran that the criminals sometimes speak on the Day of Resurrection. This is because—as we have pointed out before—there are many stations (mawaqif) on the Day of Resurrection. In some stations, the tongue ceases to function and it is the turn for the limbs to bear witness, and in others, the tongue is opened and speaks of things that are signs of intense regret, sorrow, bewilderment, and misery.

Again, at the end of this segment, it says: "Woe on that Day to the deniers!" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ‌).

In another segment, addressing the criminals as a narration of the scene of that Day, it says: "This is the Day of Sorting; We have gathered you and the former generations" (هذا يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ جَمَعْناكُمْ وَ الْأَوَّلِينَ‌).

Today, We have gathered all human beings without exception, from the first to the last, for the accounting and the settlement of disputes in this great arena and court.

"Now, if you have a plan [to escape the clutches of punishment], then use it against Me!" (فَإِنْ كانَ لَكُمْ كَيْدٌ فَكِيدُونِ‌) [^1]. Can you escape from the realm of My sovereignty? Or can you prevail over My power?

Or do you have the ability to be released by paying a ransom?

Or do you have the power to deceive the accounting officials?

Whatever you can do, do it, but know that you can do nothing! In reality, this command is what is called a "command of incapacitation" (amr ta'jizi), mentioned to clarify the helplessness and inability of the opposing party, like what has been mentioned regarding the Holy Quran: "If you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a Surah like it."

"Kayd" (rhyming with sayd), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: It is a type of resourcefulness that is sometimes blameworthy and sometimes praiseworthy, although its use in blameworthy cases is more frequent (as is the case in the verse under discussion).

Certainly, on that Day, nothing can be done by them, for we know that Day is a Day when the human hand is cut off from all means and instruments, as stated in verse 166 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "وَ تَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبابُ‌": "and all means are cut off from them."

It is noteworthy that on one hand, He says: That Day is "يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ‌", meaning the Day of Separations, and on the other hand, He says that Day is "يَوْمَ الْجَمْعِ‌", meaning the Day of Gathering. Each of these two occurs in a specific stage: first, everyone is gathered in that great court, and then, according to their beliefs and deeds, they are separated into different ranks. Even those who depart for Paradise have various ranks and different degrees, and those heading for Hell also have different ranks and depths (darakat). Yes, that Day is the Day of separating truth from falsehood, and the oppressor from the oppressed.

And again, He repeats the same threatening and awakening sentence, saying: "Woe on that Day to the deniers!" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ‌).

[^1]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn", Vol. 5, p. 49.

[^1]: The nun in "فكيدون" has a kasrah, and its kasrah stands in place of the first-person singular pronoun "ya". Originally it was "فكيدونى"; the "ya" was deleted and the kasrah remains as an indicator. The first-person pronoun, according to the apparent meaning of the verses, refers to the Pure Essence of Allah, and the possibility that it refers to the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is very remote.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى ظِلَـٰلٍ وَعُيُونٍ (٤١)
Indeed the Godwary will be amid shades and springs (41)
وَفَوَٰكِهَ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ (٤٢)
and [enjoying] such fruits as they desire. (42)
كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ هَنِيٓـًٔۢا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٤٣)
[They will be told:] ‘Enjoy your food and drink, [a reward] for what you used to do. (43)
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٤٤)
Thus do We reward the virtuous.’ (44)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٤٥)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (45)
كُلُوا۟ وَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ قَلِيلًا إِنَّكُم مُّجْرِمُونَ (٤٦)
‘Eat and enjoy a little! You are indeed guilty.’ (46)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٤٧)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (47)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱرْكَعُوا۟ لَا يَرْكَعُونَ (٤٨)
When they are told, ‘Bow down [in prayer],’ they do not bow down! (48)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٤٩)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (49)
فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ بَعْدَهُۥ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٠)
So what discourse will they believe after this? (50)

Commentary

If they do not believe in the Quran, in what discourse will they believe?!

We know that the method of the Quran is to interweave warnings with glad tidings, and threats with encouragement; likewise, it mentions the fate of the believers alongside the fate of the criminals so that through the principle of contrast, matters may be better understood.

Based on this tradition, following the description of the various punishments for the criminals on the Day of Resurrection, the above verses make a meaningful and brief reference to the state of the righteous on that day, stating: "Indeed, the righteous will be amid shades and springs" (إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي ظِلالٍ وَ عُيُونٍ).

This is while the criminals—as understood from the previous verses—are in the scorching and suffocating shade of smoke.

"Zilal" (ظلال) is the plural of "Zill" (ظل), meaning shade, whether it be shade like that of trees during the day, or the shade that occurs in the darkness of night; whereas "Fay’" (فى‌ء) refers to shade that is created only in opposition to a source of light, such as the shade of trees against the sun.

Then it adds: "And fruits from whatever they desire" (وَ فَواكِهَ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ). It is clear that the mention of "fruits," "shades," and "springs" is an allusion to a portion of the great Divine blessings bestowed upon them—a portion that can be expressed and depicted in the language of the people of this world. However, that which cannot be contained in words and does not cross the minds of the inhabitants of this world is far superior and higher than this.

Interestingly, they are hosted in this Divine guest-house in the most excellent manner, as stated in the next verse where they are told: "Eat and drink with relish for what you used to do!" (كُلُوا وَ اشْرَبُوا هَنِيئاً بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ). This sentence is either directly from Allah addressing them, or through the angels, accompanied by manifest kindness and affection, which serves as nourishment for their souls and spirits.

The expression "for what you used to do" (بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ) indicates that these blessings are not given to anyone without accountability, and they are not attained through mere claims, fantasies, or assumptions; they are provided only through righteous deeds.

"Hani’" (هنى‌ء) (on the pattern of Malih), according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, refers to anything that is not followed by hardship and does not produce discomfort; hence, wholesome food and water are called "Hani’," and sometimes it is applied to a pleasant life.

This is an indication that the fruits, foods, and drinks of Paradise are not like the food and water of this world, which sometimes leave ill effects on the body or are followed by undesirable complications.

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether this command to eat and drink is to express the permissibility of using these blessings, or if it is truly a command and instruction.

However, it should be noted that such commands given during hospitality are a type of request from the speaker expressed for the greatness and respect of the guest; the host desires that the guest eats more of his food so that he may be further honored.

In the next verse, it emphasizes again that these blessings are not without reason, adding: "Indeed, thus do We reward the doers of good" (إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ). It is interesting that in the first verse, the focus was on "Piety" (Taqwa), in the next verse on "Action" (Amal), and in this verse on "Excellence and Doing Good" (Ihsan).

"Piety" is any form of abstaining from sin, corruption, polytheism, and disbelief; "Excellence" (Ihsan) is the performance of every good deed; and "Action" refers to righteous deeds. This is to clarify that the program of Divine blessings pertains only to this group, not to the false claimants of faith and those polluted with various types of corruption, even if they outwardly appear to be among the people of faith.

At the end of this section, it repeats once more: "Woe, that Day, to the deniers" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ). Woe to those who are deprived of all these blessings and kindnesses, for the regret of this deprivation is no less painful than the scorching fire of Hell.

Since one of the factors for denying the Resurrection is indulging in the fleeting pleasures of the world and the desire for unconditional freedom to exploit these pleasures, the next verse addresses the criminals in a threatening tone, saying: "Eat and enjoy yourselves for a little [while]; indeed, you are criminals" (كُلُوا وَ تَمَتَّعُوا قَلِيلًا إِنَّكُمْ مُجْرِمُونَ). The expression "a little" (قليلا) can refer to the short duration of human life in this world, as well as the insignificance of the blessings of this world compared to the countless blessings of the Hereafter.

Although some commentators have said that this address is made to the criminals in the Hereafter, considering that in the Hereafter no enjoyment or benefit from the blessings of life is conceivable for the criminals, it must be accepted that this speech is an address to them in this world.

In reality, the "pious" and God-fearing are hosted with the utmost respect in the Hereafter and are addressed with the phrase full of kindness: "Eat and drink with relish," but the world-worshippers are addressed in this world with the threatening phrase: "Eat and enjoy yourselves for a little."

To the pious, He says: "for what you used to do" (بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ) (these are in return for the righteous deeds you performed).

And to these, He says: "indeed, you are criminals" (إِنَّكُمْ مُجْرِمُونَ): "This threat is because you are criminals." [^1] In any case, it shows that the source of Divine punishment is the crime and sin of man, which stems from lack of faith or being enslaved by desires.

Then, He completes this threat once more with the sentence: "Woe, that Day, to the deniers" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ). The very ones who were deceived and deluded by the glitter of the world and its pleasures and lusts, and thus purchased Divine punishment for themselves.

In the next verse, He points to another factor of their deviation, misery, and pollution, adding: "They are so intoxicated with the wine of arrogance that when it is said to them, 'Bow [in prayer],' they do not bow" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ارْكَعُوا لا يَرْكَعُونَ). Many commentators have said that this verse was revealed concerning the tribe of "Thaqif," to whom the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Perform the prayer." They replied: "We will never bow down to anyone, for that is a disgrace for us!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"There is no good in a religion that has no bowing (Ruku') and no prostration (Sujud)!" [^2]

They did not only refuse to bow and prostrate, but this spirit of arrogance and pride was reflected in all their thoughts and lives. They were neither submissive to God nor to the commands of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; they neither recognized the rights of the people nor had humility before the Creator or modesty before the creation. In truth, these two factors (arrogance and lust-worship) are among the most important causes of crime, sin, disbelief, injustice, and rebellion.

Some have suggested the possibility that this command "Bow" (Ark'u) will be said to them on the Day of Resurrection, but this possibility seems remote, especially considering the preceding and following verses.

And then, for the tenth and final time in this Surah, He says: "Woe, that Day, to the deniers" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ).

In the final verse under discussion, which is the last verse of Surah Al-Mursalat, He says in a tone mixed with rebuke and full of reproach, in the form of an interrogation mixed with wonder: "Then in what discourse after it [the Quran] will they believe?" (فَبِأَيِّ حَدِيثٍ بَعْدَهُ يُؤْمِنُونَ). One who does not believe in the Quran—which, if it had been sent down upon the mountains, they would have been humbled and rent asunder—will not submit to any Heavenly Book or any rational logic; and this is a sign of the spirit of enmity and stubbornness.

Key Point

As we mentioned at the beginning of the Surah, the sentence "Woe, that Day, to the deniers" is repeated ten times within it. This is to emphasize an important and shocking reality, and similar instances are abundant in the speech of the eloquent and articulate, who repeat the part upon which they have special focus and emphasis in poetry and prose.

However, some commentators believe that each of these ten verses refers to a new point related to the denial of the matters preceding it; therefore, there is no redundancy.

We conclude this Surah with a sentence from the commentary Ruh al-Bayan. He says that this Surah was revealed to the Prophet in a cave near the "Khif" Mosque in the land of "Mina," and I personally have visited this cave.

O Allah! Grant us the success never to be polluted by the denial of Your verses. O Lord! Protect us from arrogance, pride, and whims and desires, which are the primary sources of crimes.

O God! On that day when the pious are hosted with full respect in the proximity of Your presence, place us in their ranks.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. 11 Rabi' al-Thani 1407 (December 14, 1986). End of Volume 25 of Tafsir Nemouneh.

[^1]: This verse actually has an omitted part, and according to Majma' al-Bayan, its estimation is: "Eat and enjoy yourselves for a little, for death is inevitable." However, it seems more appropriate that the estimation be: "Eat and enjoy yourselves for a little and await the punishment, for you are criminals."

[^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 419; the same meaning is narrated by Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, Qurtubi in his Tafsir, Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf, and Ruh al-Bayan under the verse in question.
